narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 36—right around the typical 36.7 average for students in our dataset—and an undergraduate GPA of 2.5, which is below the usual 3.21 mark. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated an 82% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly signaled a higher risk of bar failure compared to typical students who rank around the middle of their class. Your Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced this concern, as it similarly positioned you at the lower end relative to the average fifth-decile performance, pulling the prediction further toward failure. On a brighter note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pushing the odds toward passing since it aligned closely with what successful students usually achieve. These three factors—your early and later high school rankings as the top influences, tempered by your LSAT—dominated the model's decision, outweighing other details like your full-time status, which is common for 93% of applicants. While your family's income level is a strength shared by many, it couldn't offset the weight of those lower academic deciles in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many from similar backgrounds succeed with targeted improvements—but the model prioritizes patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, focus on bolstering your academic record, perhaps through postgraduate courses or retaking key exams to climb those decile rankings, and reapply after demonstrating stronger performance. Consider programs offering bar prep support tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. With deliberate steps, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time. Your determination will be key.
